content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

June 2, 1995

Kenneth D. Rozell, Esq.
City of Dana Point
c/o Burke, Williams and Sorensen
3200 Park Center Drive, Suite 750
Costa Mesa, California 92626

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-149

Dear Mr. Rozell:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the responsibilities of City of Dana Point Councilmember
Karen Lloreda pursuant to the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

May Councilmember Lloreda participate in decisions of the
City of Dana Point regarding Birtcher Construction, a source of
income of her spouse's business?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Lloreda may not participate in any governmental
decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Birtcher Construction or other economic
interests, either directly or indirectly.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000 - 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-149
Page 2


FACTS

The Dana Point City Council will be rendering various
decisions regarding land use entitlements for the Ritz Point
project at Niguel Road and Corniche Drive. Ritz Point property is
owned by Parcel R.P., which plans to build this development.

Dana Point City Councilmember Karen Lloreda's spouse, Nick
Clarke, is the sole proprietor of Creative Woodsmith, a company
which constructs (among other things) custom cabinets. In
February 1994, Creative Woodsmith entered into a contract with
Birtcher Construction valued at approximately $65,000 to construct
custom cabinets. Within the past 12 months, Creative Woodsmith
has received payments of approximately $58,500 from Birtcher
Construction. There is a dispute between Birtcher Construction
and Creative Woodsmith regarding the remaining $6,500 due on the
contract.

Birtcher Construction has been retained by Parcel R.P. to
plan and build the Ritz Point project. Birtcher Construction has
also provided consulting services on the feasibility, design, and
planning of the project. Thus, Birtcher Construction's vice-
president has made presentations before the city's planning
commission on behalf of Parcel R.P. regarding the project.

On April 19, 1995, the city's planning commission denied
Vesting Tentative Tract Map VTTM 13104 and Site Development Permit
SDP 93-04, and recommended that the city council deny General Plan
Amendment GPA94-01, Local Coastal Program Amendment GPA95-05, Zone
Change ZC94-04 and Specific Plan SP94-01, all of which relate to
the Ritz Point project at Niguel Road and Corniche Drive.
Parcel R.P., the developer, has filed a formal appeal with the
city, concerning the planning commission's actions and
recommendations. If the city council approves the project,
Birtcher Construction will construct the 151 units at Ritz Point.
No facts are provided indicating that Creative Woodsmith will be
constructing cabinets, or doing other work, in connection with the
Ritz Point project.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. As a member of the Dana
Point City Council, Councilmember Lloreda is a public official
under the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18700.)

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,

Our File No. A-95-149
Page 3


distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the
public official has a direct or indirect
investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000)
or more.

                        * * * *

     (c)  Any source of income, other than
gifts and other than loans by a commercial
lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public
without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to
the public official within 12 months prior to
the time when the decision is made.

                        * * * *

     For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on
behalf of a public official, or by a business
entity or trust in which the official, the
official's agents, spouse, and dependent
children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.

     Income of an individual includes all income received by a
public official.  (Section 82030.)  Consequently, income which
aggregates $250 within the preceding twelve months is income of
potential disqualifying economic interest of the official with
respect to the source of the income.  In addition, income of an
individual includes any community property interest in the income
of a spouse and, in the case of income from a business entity in
which the spouse has a ten percent interest or greater, the public
official's community property interest in the spouses's pro rata
share of such income.  (Section 82030(a).)

     Under your facts, since it is the councilmember's community
property interest in her spouse's income which triggers
disqualification, the threshold level for triggering a conflict of
interest is in effect, doubled.  Thus, the threshold level is
$500.  The councilmember's spouse has received approximately
$58,500 from Birtcher Construction within the past twelve months.
Consequently, the councilmember may not participate in any
governmental decision which will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on Birtcher Construction pursuant to Section

Our File No. A-95-149
Page 4


87103(c).2

The councilmember also has an indirect investment interest in
her spouse's business, presumably in an amount of at least
$1,000.3 Thus, her spouse's business is also a potentially
disqualifying economic interest of hers within the meaning of
Section 87103(a).

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular decision. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Birtcher Construction has been retained to construct the 151
homes at the project site. Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable
that the city council decisions regarding the appeal on the
development will financially affect Birtcher Construction.

However, the financial effect on a source of income must also
be material to require disqualification. The Commission has
adopted differing guides to determine whether the effect of a
decision is material, based on the specific circumstances of each
decision. Regulation 18702.1 provides that where a source of
income is directly before an official's agency, the effect of the
decision is deemed to be material and disqualification is
required.

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides, in pertinent part:

A person or business entity is directly
involved in a decision before an official's agency
when that person or entity, either personally or by
an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;


2 In your letter, you indicate that Birtcher Construction may
also be a source of "unearned income" to the councilmember within
the meaning of Section 87103(c). Since the spouse's business has
received income from Birtcher Construction in an amount in excess
of the threshold amount during the preceding twelve months, it is
not necessary to reach a conclusion on this issue.

3 Pursuant to Section 82034, an official is considered to hold
investments held by the spouse as if the interests were held by
the official directly.

Our File No. A-95-149
Page 5


     (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

     (3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

     According to your facts, Parcel R.P. has initiated the appeal
through its legal counsel, and Parcel R.P. is the named party to
the appeal. Parcel R.P. is also the person or business entity
which is the subject of the proceeding since it involves approval
of an application submitted by Parcel R.P., the subject person or
business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).) It does not appear
that Birther Construction, has initiated the appeal, is a named
party, or is the subject of the proceeding within the meaning of
Regulation 18702.1(b). Consequently, the pending governmental
decisions will affect Birther Construction indirectly.

     When a business entity which is an economic interest of a
public official is affected indirectly by a governmental decision,
the appropriate standard for determining materiality is that of
Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed). Regulation 18702.2 provides
differing dollar thresholds of materiality depending on the
financial size of the business entity. For example, for a
relatively small business entity, Regulation 18702.2(g) provides
that the effect of a decision is material if:

          (1) The decision will result in an increase
     or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
     of $10,000 or more; or

          (2) The decision will result in the business
     entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
     reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
     fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

          (3) The decision will result in an increase
     or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
     of $10,000 or more.

     Accordingly, if subdivision (g) of Regulation 18702.2 is the
applicable subdivision and the pending decisions will have an
effect on Birther Construction in the amounts set forth above,
Councilmember Loreda must disqualify herself from participating
in the decisions concerning the appeal. You have provided no
facts to reach a conclusion on this issue. However, it would
appear that a decision concerning an appeal involving the
construction of 151 homes would materially affect the builder.

Our File No. A-95-149
Page 6


However, this is a factual determination which ultimately must be
made by the official.4

The "Public Generally" Exception

     Even if an official has an economic interest in a decision
which will be materially financially affected by the decision, the
official may still participate in the decision if the effect on
the official's economic interest is indistinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Section 87103, Regulation
18703.)  However, the regulation does not appear to apply to your
facts.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.5

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Luisa Menchaca
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure


_________________________________________________________________

4
     The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

5
     Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
